summary: This notebook provides a comprehensive feature engineering and exploratory data analysis pipeline for the NFL Big Data Bowl 2026 competition. It transforms raw player tracking CSVs into a rich feature set by integrating physical attributes, kinematic physics, play context, and spatial awareness metrics. The author demonstrates how domain-specific knowledge—such as trigonometric directional encoding, momentum calculations, and ball-landing proximity—can be systematically derived from frame-by-frame tracking data. Readers should take away a reusable architectural pattern for merging input/output tracking data, structuring feature generation by dependency order, and validating new features through baseline model importance and targeted visualizations. The notebook emphasizes that spatial relationships and role-based behavior are more predictive than raw movement statistics alone.
competition_flow: Loads weekly NFL Big Data Bowl 2026 tracking CSVs, merges input and output data on game/play/player/frame identifiers, applies a multi-stage feature engineering pipeline combining physics, spatial, and contextual metrics, and outputs engineered features as Parquet files alongside comprehensive EDA visualizations and baseline feature importance scores.
data_reading: Reads weekly input and output CSV files via pd.read_csv() from the competition input directory.
Merges input tracking data with output target coordinates on game_id, play_id, nfl_id, and frame_id.
Persists engineered features as .parquet files with accompanying JSON metadata for reproducibility.
data_processing: Converts height strings to inches and one-hot encodes categorical positions and roles.
Applies sin/cos trigonometric transformations to orientation and direction columns to handle circular continuity.
Bins yardline numbers into field thirds and computes Euclidean distances to ball landing spots, sidelines, and endzones.
Groups tracking data by play and player to calculate maximum kinematic metrics per sequence.
Imputes missing values with medians for baseline modeling.
insights: Trigonometric encoding of orientation and direction prevents artificial discontinuities at 0°/360° boundaries.
Distance to the ball landing spot strongly correlates with receiver and defender route adjustments.
Player awareness metrics (orientation toward QB or targeted receiver) provide actionable spatial context beyond raw coordinates.
Momentum and kinetic energy features capture physical constraints that raw speed and acceleration miss.
Field position (yardline/third) fundamentally shifts route concepts and target location distributions.
Grouping tracking data by play to extract maximum kinematic metrics reveals player capability ceilings that frame-level data obscures.
